description: 2-topic conversation, one clean switch, no return - baseline case

turns.question: Under which circumstances can a concern or complaint be made under this University of Essex whistleblowing policy?
turns.retrieval_query: Under which circumstances can a concern or complaint be made under this University of Essex whistleblowing policy?
turns.answer:
Under the University of Essex Whistleblowing Policy, concerns or complaints can be made if they involve serious wrongdoing that has an impact beyond the individual making the disclosure. Specifically:

- The concern must be in the public interest.
- It should tend to show malpractice.
- Disclosure is made in accordance with these procedures.

The policy protects individuals who make such disclosures from detriment provided certain conditions are met, as detailed in sections 9 and 13 of the document. Personal matters or financial/business decisions not involving malpractice are outside the scope of this policy. Sources: [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf]

turns.question: Who can someone raise that concern with?
turns.retrieval_query: Who can someone raise a concern or complaint with under the University of Essex whistleblowing policy?
turns.answer:
According to the University of Essex Whistleblowing Policy, concerns or complaints should be raised in the following ways:

- For employees, the initial step is to raise any concerns with their line manager or formally with the University Secretary.
- For students, if the concern is not personal and in the public interest, it must be raised under the Code of Student Conduct or the Student Concerns and Complaints Procedure. Individuals unsure about which procedure to use should contact People & Culture.

Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf]
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/student-concerns-complaints-procedure.pdf]

turns.question: What constitutes plagiarism according to the academic offences procedure?
turns.retrieval_query: What constitutes plagiarism according to the University of Essex academic offences procedure?
turns.answer:
According to the University of Essex Academic Offences Procedure, plagiarism is defined as using or copying someone else's work (whether written, printed, or in any other form) without proper acknowledgment in any assignment, examination, or other assessed work. An example provided is copying a paragraph from a book without citing the original author.

Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/academic-offences-procedure.pdf]

turns.question: What penalties can be applied for that?
turns.retrieval_query: What penalties can be applied for plagiarism according to the University of Essex Academic Offences Procedure?
turns.answer:
For plagiarism, the following penalties may be applied by Academic Offences Committees:

- **Penalty 2:** The Examiners shall be instructed to consider the non-plagiarised sections of the final submission, for the degree for which it has been submitted.
- **Penalty 3:** No longer eligible for full award: The Examiners shall be instructed to consider the non-plagiarised sections of the final submission for a lesser award only.
- **Penalty 4:** Required to withdraw with no qualification awarded.

These penalties apply specifically to allegations that have occurred prior to the student's final assessment. Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/academic-offences-procedures-2017-18.pdf]
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/academic-offences-procedure.pdf]

turns.question: What does the Fitness to Study policy address in terms of student behavior?
turns.retrieval_query: What does the Fitness to Study policy address in terms of student behavior?
turns.answer:
The Fitness to Study policy addresses situations where a student's health, mental health, or behavior causes concern and impacts their ability to engage with studies or disrupts others. This includes behaviors within accommodation that affect other students or staff.

Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/fitness-to-study.pdf]

turns.question: What does the term 'Capped Mark' mean in the undergraduate rules of assessment?
turns.retrieval_query: What does the term 'Capped Mark' mean in the undergraduate rules of assessment?
turns.answer:
In the context of the University of Essex's undergraduate rules of assessment for the academic year 2025-26, a Capped Mark when reassessment is offered means that the maximum mark that will be recorded for the Module Aggregate is 40. The actual mark achieved will still be used to determine whether the assessment has been passed and will appear on your transcript.

Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/glossary/roa-ug-glossary.pdf]

turns.question: Going back to my earlier question about student behavior concerns - who initiates that process?
turns.retrieval_query: Who initiates the process for addressing student behavior concerns under the Fitness to Study policy?
turns.answer:
The process can be initiated by staff members if they have concerns about a student's health, mental health, or behavior that impacts their ability to study. Staff may approach the student directly with any concerns, seeking guidance from the Student Wellbeing and Inclusivity Service on how best to do this confidentially.

Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/fitness-to-study.pdf]

turns.retrieval_query: What is the maximum number of credits that can be carried forward for progression in the MSc Periodontology programme?
turns.answer:
The maximum number of credits that can be carried forward for progression in the MSc Periodontology programme is 30 credits.

turns.retrieval_query: Under which circumstances can a concern or complaint be made under this University of Essex whistleblowing policy?
turns.answer:
Under the University of Essex Whistleblowing Policy, concerns or complaints can be made if they involve serious wrongdoing that has an impact beyond the individual making the disclosure. Specifically:

- The concern must be in the public interest.
- It should tend to show malpractice.
- Disclosure is made in accordance with these procedures.

The policy protects individuals who make such disclosures from detriment provided certain conditions are met, as detailed in sections 9 and 13 of the document. Personal matters or financial/business decisions not involving malpractice are outside the scope of this policy. turns.question: What does the Fitness to Study policy address in terms of student behavior?
turns.retrieval_query: What does the Fitness to Study policy address in terms of student behavior?
turns.answer:
The Fitness to Study policy addresses situations where a student's health, mental health, or behavior causes concern and may impact their studies, accommodation, or the wellbeing of others. Specifically, it covers scenarios where:

- A student’s behavior within accommodation is causing difficulties for other students or staff.
- The student’s actions are disrupting teaching, learning, or support provided to other students.
- There are doubts about the student's fitness to study at the University and/or within a placement, even after reasonable adjustments have been made.
- The student's health problems present a risk to themselves or others.

The policy ensures a coordinated response involving various university departments and supports staff in identifying when referral to external agencies is necessary. Sources: [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/fitness-to-study.pdf]

turns.question: How long do MBA students have to complete their studies at Essex?
turns.retrieval_query: How long do MBA students have to complete their studies at Essex?
turns.answer:
MBA students at the University of Essex are required to complete their studies within 5 years. This timeframe is due to rules set by accrediting bodies and is specified in the MBA Rules of Assessment document for the 2025-26 academic year.

Source: [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/pgt/2025-26/department-specific/mba-25.pdf]

turns.question: What constitutes plagiarism according to the academic offences procedure?
turns.retrieval_query: What constitutes plagiarism according to the University of Essex Academic Offences Procedure?
turns.answer:
According to the University of Essex Academic Offences Procedure, plagiarism is defined as using or copying the work of others (whether written, printed, or in any other form) without proper acknowledgement in any assignment, examination, or other assessed work. This includes:

- Copying a paragraph from a book without citing the original author.
- Reusing parts of a previous assignment without acknowledging the original source.

turns.question: What happens if a student exceeds that trailing credit limit?
turns.retrieval_query: What happens if a student exceeds the 30-credit trailing limit for the MSc Periodontology or Postgraduate Diploma in Periodontology programmes?
turns.answer:
If a student exceeds the trailing credit limit (30 credits), they will not be able to carry forward additional failed credits. The University of Essex policies indicate that students are permitted to progress while trailing a maximum of 30 credits, meaning these credits can be reassessed in the next academic year. Exceeding this limit would likely result in the student being required to address all outstanding failures before progressing or completing their program.
